text: In this video we will demonstrate technical aspects of robotic assisted left upper lobectomy for squamous cell carcinoma. The video illustrates robotic assisted technique when encounter with challenging anatomy of upper pulmonary artery branches.

A sixty-one year old male initially presented with dyspnea on exertion and was found to have a two point six centimeters left upper lob mass. On biopsy, this was found to be squamous cell carcinoma. His PET-CT and IBIS were negative for lymph node disease. Therefore, he was staged T-I-C-N-Zero-M-Zero clinical stage I-A.
This is his imaging showing the left upper lobe speculated perihelion mass. PET-CT showing SUV uptake.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position with arms suspended. Four 8mm robotic ports are placed in the eighth intercostal space along the midclavicular, anterior axillary, posterior axillary, and perispinal lines. An additional 15mm assistant port is positioned at the subcostal margin along the anterior axillary line.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to dissect the inferior pulmonary ligament. A level nine lymph node is identified and dissected out, followed by the dissection of a level seven lymph node pocket. The anterior oblique fissure is divided using a stapler and gentle blunt dissection.

The surgeon identifies and dissects out a level XI lymph node located in the oblique fissure. A safe passage is visible from the interlobar fissure to the posterior hilum. The inferior lingual branch of the pulmonary artery is identified within the interlobar fissure, circumferentially controlled by blunt dissection, and divided. The superior lingual artery is also divided.

The bedside assistant introduces a wide load stapler through the posterior port. The surgeon completes the division of the posterior fissure using purple staple loads through the anterior port. The apico-posterior branch of the pulmonary artery is identified, circumferentially controlled, and divided.

The surgeon retracts the upper lobe posteriorly to isolate and divide the superior pulmonary vein, as the anatomical features prevent identification of the anterior branch of the pulmonary artery at this stage.

The surgeon divides the left upper lobe bronchus using electrocautery, providing clear exposure of the anterior branch of the pulmonary artery for safe division. The left upper bronchial stem is then closed with a running IIIO absorbable V-lock suture.

The surgeon places the left upper lobe in a specimen retrieval bag and removes it through the incision and the 15mm assistant port. After specimen removal, a multilevel intercostal block is performed, and a 28 French chest tube is inserted through the camera port incision. The lung is inflated, and the remaining incisions are closed.

This patient's post-operative course was complicated by COVID-Nineteen infection, respiratory failure and prolonged air leak. He was intubated with subsequent tracheostomy and prolonged hospital stay. He was discharged to the nursing facility on post-operative day number forty-four. However, this complication was not related to the surgical technique itself.

The surgeon uses fully articulated instruments to perform precise dissection around an unusual branch of the anterior segmental artery, carefully avoiding vessel injuries and other potential intraoperative mistakes.
